data.title: ARKQ: AI's Renaissance Lifting Cathie Wood's Autonomous Tech & Robotics ETF (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The AI trade is back in apparent full force. Chip stocks have soared, up 18 days in a row, heading into the final days of April. That industry's strength has helped lift the S&P 500 to a record high, even amid geopolitical tensions between the US and Iran, along with $100 oil. But it's not just semiconductors fueling the market's incline. Broader tech, industrials, and AI infrastructure plays are on a heater after a cold start to 2026.

Enter the ARK Autonomous Technology & Robotics ETF (ARKQ). I was neutral on the fund back in February of 2025. Among Cathie Wood's lesser-known products, it struggled into early April of last year, but then took flight after Liberation Day. A correction beginning in January culminated in a fierce selloff at the end of last month. But animal spirits are alive and well once again.

Today, I am upgrading ARKQ to a buy from a hold. I recently underscored a buy call on Tesla (TSLA), and that is ARKQ's largest position. While the ETF's valuation is lofty, dips are being bought, and rising EPS estimates are buttressed by solid technicals.

ARKQ's Rally Indicative of AI Tailwinds, Up Big YoY

According to the issuer, ARKQ seeks long-term capital growth by investing, under normal circumstances, primarily (at least 80% of its assets) in domestic and foreign equity securities of autonomous technology and robotics companies relevant to the fund's investment theme of disruptive innovation. The ETF aims to own companies focused on developing new products or services, technological improvements, and advancements in scientific research related to, among other things, energy, automation and manufacturing, materials, artificial intelligence, and transportation.

ARKQ is a medium-sized ETF, now with $2.7 billion in assets under management as of April 24, 2026. That's up from $992 million at the time of my previous analysis. Shares are up 57% since my Q1 2026 assessment, and ARKQ's current dividend yield is low at 24 basis points, while its annual expense ratio is elevated at 75 basis points.

Share-price momentum is exceptional right now; however, the fund earns a pristine A+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha's quantitative scoring system. Risk is another story. High historical standard deviation trends and a concentrated and thematic portfolio make weekly swings severe.

Finally, on liquidity, ARKQ scores well. Average daily volume is north of 200,000 shares, while its median 30-day bid/ask spread is 8 basis points. I would still encourage prospective investors to use limit orders, particularly around the market open.

For an update on the portfolio, the 4-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar is concentrated toward the upper-right corner of the style box, indicating high weight to global large-cap growth shares. A small, but material, 13.4% of the fund is non-US, offering some geographic diversification. There's also a decent mix of large, mid, and small-cap access.

The valuation is still not all that appealing to me at more than 36x earnings. Paired with a long-term EPS growth rate of 17.3%, the PEG is above 2. Keep in mind that TSLA commands a high weight, which naturally puts upward pressure on the P/E.

ARKQ: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming into the sector breakdown, Industrials actually carries the largest weight at just under 40%. AI-related cyclicals such as Krato Defense & Security Solutions (KTOS) and Deere & Co. (DE) complement more tech-centered stocks like Tesla (TSLA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD).

Palantir (PLTR) is sort of a bridge between the aerospace & defense and TMT-related areas.

ARKQ: Tesla is the Largest Position, Heavy In Industrials & Tech

Seasonally, we are about to begin a very bullish stretch. Over the past 10 years, May through August has been exceptionally bullish, which is a key factor in my rating upgrade.

ARKQ: Bullish May-August Calendar Trends

The Technical Take

Sporting a premium valuation and with sanguine seasonals on tap, ARKQ's technical situation is constructive. Notice in the chart below that shares recently broke out of a bull flag pattern after notching an all-time high in January. That peak occurred on rather heavy volume, resulting in a high amount of shares traded near today's level. So, it's possible that a consolidation period unfolds before the $135 zenith is taken out.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It's on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. A minor uptrend support line from the lows of Q3 last year bolsters the uptrend's case. What's more, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart confirms the price upswing, as it broke out from its own downtrend.

There is a pair of gaps below today's price, which is a near-term bearish technical risk.

ARKQ: Pressing Up Against Record Highs, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on ARKQ. I was too timid on the autonomous tech & robotics portfolio early last year, and getting long a few months after my analysis has paid off. Despite the premium P/E, I see upward momentum continuing.

data.title: Alto Ingredients Going Full Soprano
data.description:
DOMO Capital Historical ALTO Update

It has been a long time since I published my first Alto Ingredients (ALTO) article on May 2nd, 2023, when it traded at $1.27 per share: "Alto Ingredients Growth Story Temporarily Lost In A 'Maize'"

Wow, a lot has happened since then! Shares are currently trading about 300% higher (>$5 a share)... not bad for a three-year return. Of course, then there was also the bullish article in October of 2023 at around $4.20 a share. Although shares are higher today, investors had to first experience a stomach-churning decline to $0.77. Over this time there were a lot of things I was wrong about, some things I was right about, unbelievable bad luck, and now... incredibly good luck.

As most of you are aware, DOMO Capital, where I am the sole owner and portfolio manager, was able to take advantage of the opportunity and significantly add to the ALTO position in the low $1s, leading to a 13G filing in January of 2025 showing ownership of 5,420,069 shares, representing a 7.1% ownership in the company. Although portfolio rules required some trimming at the end of 2025, I did not lose our conviction in ALTO or the ethanol industry in general, which led me to also build a substantial position in Green Plains (GPRE) as ALTO was trimmed.

The Deluge

From 2022 through early 2025, ALTO has had some of the worst luck of any public company.

How bad?

Just when things were looking great in 2022, Russia invaded Ukraine (a major corn exporter), which sent corn basis rocketing and crush margins tanking. Drought in the US led to sustained higher corn basis, especially out West, which hurt ALTO more than other ethanol producers. Additionally, specialty alcohol became more commoditized after COVID, and lower specialty alcohol premiums again hit ALTO harder than other ethanol producers that focused more on renewable fuel.

During these years there was also an extraordinary spike in natural gas prices out West that, once again, specifically hurt ALTO, as they were the only major ethanol producer in that area. To make matters worse, ALTO spent over $20M on a major project at Magic Valley aimed at making the plant sustainably profitable; however, by the time the project was completed (after several lengthy delays due to design flaws), the unforeseen, sustained, higher corn basis meant that the plant would still be unprofitable even with the upgrades. The $20M in CapEx loss was only compounded by the losses from trying to run the plant during the upgrades in highly unprofitable economic conditions.

At one point, ALTO had a fire that hurt production at the worst possible time, the most profitable 3rd quarter, and then last year there was a flood in the spring that wiped out ALTO's only dock.

I wish I could say this was an exhaustive list, but it's not. There were inflation pressures that resulted in the cost of potentially very lucrative projects more than doubling. There were setbacks with ALTO's opportunity to sequester oil, where, despite being in the most beneficial geographical location in the United States, the IL legislature somehow decided to use an outdated map that inaccurately placed ALTO's sequestration site within an aquifer, which essentially killed the project.

The CEO, Bryon McGregor, likes to use the phrase "from your mouth to God's ears," but for whatever reason... God was clearly choosing vengeance rather than the answering of prayers!

However, perhaps the flood should have been a sign. Just as God used the flood to purge the world of pervasive corruption, allowing for a fresh start and a new covenant, it appears that perhaps the flood that swept ALTO's dock down the Mississippi River was the final "cleansing" required to turn the tide and usher in a streak of epic blessings!

After all, God answers prayers in His time. Not ours.

New Beginnings

And just like that... the flood brought along almost immediate new beginnings...

ALTO had both insurance on the dock as well as business interruption insurance. Therefore, after the initial deductible was paid, ALTO not only received money to build not one but two entirely new docks but also received money for the costs above and beyond their ordinary costs of running their business by having to utilize other docks/barges/trucking.

To be fair, however, prior to the flood, management did do two very important things.

1) They announced significant cost cuts:

Before I discuss the quarter, I'd like to highlight the cost-cutting steps we've taken to strengthen our financial results. As Brian mentioned, we cold-idled the Magic Valley facility, rationalized our Eagle Alcohol operations, and reduced our headcount by 16% to align with our smaller company footprint. In aggregate, our workforce reductions are expected to lower our annual costs by $7.8 million. The savings are split 74% in cost of goods sold or COGS and 26% in SG&A. We expect to realize the full financial benefit beginning in Q2. These are meaningful reductions. To put it into perspective, if we applied these measures retroactively to 2024, all other things being equal, adjusted EBITDA would have been positive for the year.

2) They also announced the purchase of Kodiak Carbonic (which they renamed to ALTO Carbonic) for $7.25 million. The nice version of my first thought when I saw that headline was... "You have got to be kidding me!" However, after a bit of digging, including conversations with previous Kodiak Carbonic employees, it very quickly became clear that ALTO pulled off a masterstroke. There are so many brilliant aspects of the deal. First, the plant was originally built for $7M back in 2015, which means that ALTO essentially purchased the plant at the 2015 cost. In my opinion, ALTO's less than ideal financial situation at the time directly contributed to their ability to negotiate an incredible deal, as they likely told Kodiak that they could either sell at the $7.25M price or ALTO would simply either shut down the plant or sell it to another party that would no longer use it to produce ethanol. Either option would have made the Kodiak Carbonic CO₂ plant worthless. After ALTO completed the acquisition, they then went and amended the terms of the agreement with the buyer of the liquefied CO₂ to lock in even greater profit over a long-term offtake agreement. Brilliant!

Per my conversations, I believe that the CO2 plant was netting over $5M a year on a standalone basis (before the amended offtake agreement). ALTO noted at the time that the payback was less than two years, and in Q4 they stated:

We continue to benefit from our Carbonic acquisition, which we completed at the beginning of 2025 as it contributed $1.4 million to our Western Production segment during the quarter.

On an annual basis this would lead to over $5M. However, the benefit to ALTO extends well beyond this, because in the past ALTO would have to constantly idle the plant when crush margins weren't strong. By owning the CO2 plant, ALTO can keep the plant running in almost any condition and benefits from lower costs on the ethanol side from avoiding the constant shutdown/restarting of the plant, but they also benefit on the CO2 side by being able to produce even more CO2 than in the past. Previously, this wouldn't make sense because they didn't profit from producing the CO2 - Kodiak did.

The easiest way to visualize this reality is to simply look at the financials. In 2024, the Western plants had a gross loss of $20 million. In 2025, they had a gross profit of $3.6M! The $3.6M figure also excludes 45z tax credits of around $4M that the plant generated in 2025. In total, this is a $27.6M annual swing that is only going to become more profitable on an annual basis in the future. This is a permanent economic change to ALTO's earnings moving forward and is not a one-time temporary benefit.

Due to the significant 2025 improvement, ALTO announced on the last earnings call that they have made significant debt payments:

We used $22 million in our financing activities as we paid down $16 million on our operating line of credit and $5 million on our term debt. As a result, we ended the year with $55 million outstanding on our term loan. At year-end, we had a total loan borrowing availability of $102 million, consisting of $37 million under our operating line of credit and $65 million under our term loan facility.

Our improved profitability in the last half of 2025 allowed us to further pay down $10 million of principal on our term debt in February of 2026, and we expect to pay down an additional $6 million in March. This will reduce the principal amount of our term debt to $39 million by the end of the first quarter. We are pleased to significantly reduce our debt and continue to strengthen our balance sheet.

Note that ALTO expected to pay debt down by another $16M during the first quarter of 2026 and before there was any news of a conflict with Iran. I would expect further debt reduction throughout 2026. As a reminder, the term loan is at a rate of 10%; therefore, the company is already set to save over $2M in 2026 on interest payments alone!

Exports

ALTO has shown a consistent ability to find the highest margin markets for their products. In the past, specialty alcohol contributed significantly to ALTO's bottom line. Although this is still a huge market for ALTO, the margins have compressed in recent years, which drove ALTO to find opportunities for even greater margin overseas. This new export market opportunity started slowly in 2025 before accelerating at the end of the year. On the Q4 earnings call, ALTO management had the following to say:

An increase in renewable fuel export sales at premiums to domestic sales contributed $5 million on a higher volume and higher average sales price per gallon.

We have contracted to sell a significant volume of renewable fuel exports for the first half of 2026 and believe there are increasing opportunities for us to expand volumes and premiums in this market. We are on track to match our 2025 high-quality alcohol volumes.

Therefore, I expect exports to continue to meaningfully contribute to ALTO's bottom line throughout 2026. The key point, however, is that ALTO has shown the ability to consistently rediscover higher-margin opportunities for their products in a way that is differentiated from competitors.

2025 Corn Harvest

While there have been some fears about the availability of fertilizer due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, this doesn't appear to be a problem that will impact ALTO anytime soon. The 2025 corn harvest set an all-time record for both national yield (186.5 bushels per acre) and total production (17 billion bushels produced). The prior year's harvest (in this case 2025) is what drives crush margins for the following year (2026). Earlier, we discussed how ALTO had incredibly bad luck with both drought and the Russian/Ukraine war, resulting in a shortage of corn and a steep corn basis. Now, in 2026, this has completely flipped into incredibly good luck with both a record corn harvest and the Iran conflict.

As an aside for those unaware. Corn basis is the cash cost to have corn delivered. This is based on the availability of corn. Generally speaking, higher corn "prices" are beneficial to ethanol producers, because ethanol tends to follow corn higher with no impact on crush margin, while co-products such as DDG and corn oil tend to rise in price along with corn, resulting in greater margins for ethanol producers. Ethanol prices, however, do not historically move much to reflect higher (or lower) corn basis.

The record 2025 corn harvest has led to a sustained low corn basis (due to huge corn stocks/inventory) that isn't going to be reversed anytime soon. Furthermore, current planting trends are in line with or even better than historical averages, as most farmers already had the fertilizer they needed to plant corn in 2026. Therefore, the very earliest any fertilizer shortage impact may hit corn availability in the US is 2028, although it's improbable that the fertilizer issue will not be resolved by the 2027 planting season.

2026 Crush Margins and Co-product Yields

As many of you are aware, I have tracked ALTO's crush margins on a daily basis since 2023 by comparing their daily corn bids against the CBOT price of Chicago ethanol.

This approach has pluses and minuses. For one, the volume that ALTO exports overseas sells for a premium to renewable fuel ethanol sold domestically. The specialty alcohol also sells at a premium. Where this tracking model becomes impaired is for the western plants. The western plants have a significantly higher cost basis (due to the distance that the corn has to be transported via rail). The western plant corn basis is not available on a daily basis, which is why the company has reported results significantly worse than I expected in previous years. Now that Magic Valley is idled and Columbia is profitable on an annual basis, thanks to the Kodiak Carbonic acquisition, the importance of tracking crush margins has become more meaningful.

Q1 Expectations

Avg. Pekin Crush 2025 Avg. Pekin Crush 2026
January $0.05 $0.03
February $0.06 $0.12
March $0.11 $0.28
Q1 Average $0.07 $0.14

Source: DOMO Capital

It's important to remember that Q1 is the seasonally worst quarter for ethanol companies. More often than not, the crush margin is actually double-digit negative. This year's crush margin ended up being about 7 cents better YOY on average at a positive $0.14. On the Q4 earnings call, the company stated:

As a reminder, the first quarter is a seasonally challenging period for us. And in January of this year, extreme cold weather disrupted River Logistics and curtailed production at our Pekin campus. We took advantage of the downtime to make some of the repairs we had planned to be completed in the second quarter during our biennial wet mill outage. This has allowed us to defer the remaining work until the spring of 2027 and to make up for January's lost production next quarter.

Nobody should be expecting positive Q1 EPS.

However, we should be expecting significant YOY improvement, and if ALTO can somehow at least announce that Q1 has positive EBITDA, that would be incredible.

A few things to consider regarding how Q1 performance could shape up:

The Pekin plant was shut down at the most beneficial time period from an annual perspective. This will boost annual earnings (specifically in Q2) as the plant will instead be running at a time when crush margins are significantly higher. In early January of 2026, the crush was actually negative at some points.

The cold weather that caused issues with the river also resulted in a short-term 1-2 week spike in natural gas costs. My understanding is that natural gas did not spike in price out west; therefore, Columbia should not have been impacted as much. As the plant was idled for a week, the impact of the natural gas spike was likely less material; however, it's something to note as a potential headwind to Q1 results. Outside this temporary spike, natural gas prices have mostly been lower YOY.

ALTO's cost cuts were not fully impacted until Q3; therefore, this should still be a YOY benefit for both Q1 and Q2.

I expect 45z credits to be realized quarterly moving forward, which will have a material YOY benefit to each quarter in 2026 until Q4 is lapped. Additionally, I expect news regarding the sale of the 45z credits the company previously announced. If the credits were sold at a higher price than anticipated, then this could further boost results. ALTO's figures show that they expect to net 80% of the gross 45z credit.

Unlike the Russian/Ukraine war, the Iranian conflict has had a materially positive economic impact for ethanol producers. The rise in oil prices (as well as the desire for cheaper fuel) has led to not only a rise in ethanol prices but also a rise in demand for ethanol. Corn prices have moved in the opposite direction since the war, which is the key differentiator here. Not only have corn prices fallen, but so has corn basis. This has resulted in incredibly, unusually high crush margins for this time of year starting in March (as you can see in the previously provided table).

Q2 Expectations

This high crush margin trend has continued into Q2 and shows no sign of slowing. Despite the higher crush margins, ethanol inventories remain lower year over year; therefore, this should not have any material impact on future crush margins. In other words, I wouldn't expect crush margins to suddenly tank if the conflict were to end because the market isn't currently being flooded with unused product.

So far, for April, the crush margin has averaged $0.36. To put into perspective just how beneficial this is, consider that in 2022 the average April crush margin was -$0.16! In 2023, 2024, and 2025, the crush for April, based on DOMO Capital's proprietary data, averaged $0.07, $0.08, and $0.11.

Assuming that ALTO produces and sells approximately 20 million gallons of ethanol a month... this would translate into an extra $5M in net profit YOY just based off of April's increased crush margin of an additional $0.25 per gallon.

However, the good news doesn't stop there. The war has also resulted in a spike in soybean oil due to the price of diesel. Corn oil trades at around a $3 to $5 premium to soybean oil. The price of DDG has also rocketed. Corn oil and DDG are two major co-products of ethanol production. Corn oil may actually be the best-performing commodity YTD! The USDA produces a weekly National Ethanol Report showing the prices, by state, YOY.

As you can see from this report (dated April 24, 2026), corn oil prices in Illinois are up 52.7% YOY, while DDG prices are up 28.6% YOY. The impact of this on ALTO's bottom line cannot be understated.

Natural gas prices have also traded roughly 30% lower YOY during April as well, and since natural gas is one of the largest costs after corn, this will also provide material benefit to ALTO's bottom line in Q2.

Therefore, while Q1 is likely to be negative (but better YOY), Q2 could very well be a blow-out quarter if crush margins continue to hold their ground. Don't forget that the Pekin plant will not have to shut down during Q2 since it was shut down in January instead. Crush margins are currently in line with a typical Q3, when crush naturally rises due to the busy summer driving season. I expect as we near Q3 that crush margins will continue to rise to levels that are significantly higher YOY. The trend is unlikely to change even with an end to the Iran conflict.

E15 Game-Changers

In late 2025, California stunned the world when legislation was approved and Governor Newsom signed AB30 into law, which approved year-round E15 in California. Previously, California was the only state in the United States that did not allow any E15 sales. This new law is expected to increase ethanol demand by over 600 million gallons per year!

The bad news is that, unfortunately, the fire marshal is currently preventing gas stations from taking advantage of this new law; however, a resolution is expected imminently. The good news, though, is that as soon as the issue is resolved, there will be a major drawdown of ethanol inventories that will further support crush margins.

ALTO is uniquely positioned as one of the only ethanol producers on the west coast and recently had this to say about the bill:

The newly signed California Assembly Bill 30 authorizing E15 fuel cells year-round in California unlocks significant demand for domestically produced ethanol. California is the largest market to allow E15 blends, adding potentially over 600 million additional gallons per year. AB30 expands consumer choice for lower carbon, cheaper fuels in California during a time when the refineries are being idled and gasoline capacity in California is tightening. With our marketing and distribution services on the West Coast, Alto is well positioned to help fill the gap.

Furthermore, it sounds like the passage of year-round E15 nationwide is becoming more and more likely. As ALTO has highlighted in recent presentations, this legislation could lead to a 5 to 7 BILLION gallon increase in ethanol demand. Trump has been adamant that he wants to see the legislation on his desk and that he would immediately sign it.

The path forward is becoming more clear. New E15 legislation was just added as a potential amendment to the Farm Bill in the House. If the amendment is approved and the Farm Bill passes, that's where it gets interesting. It seems that, currently, it would be very difficult for the Senate to pass a Farm Bill. However, once the House approves the E15 amendment and sends it over to the Senate... the Senate could approve the same E15 amendment on any legislation. It doesn't have to be the same Farm Bill. It could be funding for Iran. Anything. I believe the odds of year-round E15 getting through Congress in 2026 are over 75%. Passage would ensure a golden decade for ethanol producers.

45z Credits

During 2025, the ethanol industry benefited from several unexpected changes to 45z credits.

1) The "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" that was signed on July 4th, 2025, codified the exclusion of ILUC to all clean fuels produced after December 31, 2025. This was completely unexpected, and it's important to keep in mind that ALTO has not received a single benefit from this act yet.

In 2025, ALTO recorded a $7.5M "net" gain from 45z credits (remember this could be increased if the fee to sell the credits is less than expected). Due to the ILUC change, this credit will double in 2026 and beyond. ALTO will now net $15M a year going forward. Additionally, since ALTO is expanding the Pekin dry-mill capacity by ~5 million gallons later in 2026, this will add another $1M gross / $800,000 net (based on how ALTO is currently calculating gross vs. net credits) in 45z credits annually.

2) The "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" also unexpectedly explicitly mandated the use of the 45CF-GREET model, which includes the dedicated modules for cover crops and other farm-level carbon sequestration techniques. In February of 2026, the IRS finally issued proposed regulations that formally integrated these changes. ALTO has the potential to significantly lower their Carbon Intensity score even further by working with farmers to ensure traceability and proof of practice. Therefore, we may see a material increase to the current $15-$16M net 45z credit guidance before year-end.

3) Once again, very unexpectedly, on February 3rd, 2026, the IRS issued proposed regulations that explicitly cleared up ambiguity regarding RECs. RECs are Renewable Energy Credits. The IRS confirmed that ethanol plants could use RECs to zero out their grid electricity emissions. This means that ALTO could purchase RECs to further reduce their carbon intensity score. If ALTO, for example, is very close to getting $0.30 per gallon instead of $0.20 per gallon, they could purchase some RECs and, using the dry mill at Pekin as an example, receive $18.5M gross ($15.6M net) vs. $13M gross ($10.4M net). In short, if the RECs cost $5M or less, it would be profitable to purchase them. They could then do the same thing at their Columbia plant.

ALTO provides a table in their newest presentations that helps to visualize this, and you can see how valuable it would be for a plant at a 37.5 CI score to get to a 37.4 CI score. The REC cost to do so would be minuscule:

Additional Near-Term Catalysts

Russell 2000 Index Inclusion

At over $5 a share, ALTO is a very easy lock to be included in the Russell 2000 Index. Rank Day is April 30th. ALTO is not currently in any index, and being added to the Russell 2000 will lead to a huge increase in demand for shares from institutions. Trading over $5 a share, just generally speaking, is also very helpful to gain the interest of institutional shareholders. While Rank Day is April 30th, the official announcement of additions to the index is not expected until May 22nd. The newly reconstituted index will take effect after US market close on June 26th. You can find more info here.

Sale of Magic Valley

The company confirmed on the last earnings call that Magic Valley is still for sale. I suspect a sale of the facility could generate $20M to $40M for ALTO, depending on the buyer. I would encourage them to complete a sale and move on rather than attempt to make the plant profitable, as there are considerable opportunities available at the Columbia and Pekin locations with high ROI.

Official CO₂ Plan for Pekin

With ALTO's CO₂ sequestration plans at Pekin all but dead, the company announced last quarter that:

We're also assessing large-scale CO2 utilization and sequestration opportunities at our Pekin campus and developing plans to capture more value for our CO2 as quickly as possible.

While non-sequestration CO₂ opportunities may not provide as much money in the long run, they could provide significant sums of money in the short run along with significantly lower (perhaps zero) project costs. The ability to quickly generate material profit from the sale of CO₂ at Pekin (vs. the sequestration of the CO₂) would, without question, provide much more immediate benefit to shareholders and propel shares significantly higher. I believe this is the direction that ALTO should and will proceed, and any detailed news along these lines would be an immediate catalyst for the share price.

Conclusion

In conclusion, 2025 represented a major turnaround year for ALTO, and 2026 is likely to be a year of incredible growth due to additional export volume at high margins, a doubling (or maybe tripling) of 45z credits, additional cost cutting, increased production capacity, lower natural gas costs, and significantly higher prices for corn oil and DDG, as well as materially higher crush margins due to the Iran conflict. If crush margins can sustain current levels throughout Q2 and then increase as they typically do in Q3, then a double-digit share price for ALTO is probable.

data.title: Cushman & Wakefield: An AI-Driven Selloff Creates Opportunity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Riverwater Small Cap Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary.

Cushman & Wakefield (CWK) is one of the world's largest commercial real estate (CRE) services firms, operating in over 60 countries with approximately 52,000 employees. The company provides a comprehensive suite of services—leasing, capital markets advisory, property and facilities management, and valuation—to both occupiers and investors across the office, industrial, retail, life sciences, and data center sectors. Despite strong operational fundamentals and an improving earnings trajectory, CWK shares have traded under persistent pressure due to market concerns that artificial intelligence and technology-driven platforms could disintermediate traditional CRE service providers. We believe this fear is significantly overstated. CEO Michelle MacKay has emphasized that Cushman's key differentiator lies in delivering "tailored, bespoke solutions" through deep client relationships and human expertise—capabilities that cannot be easily replicated by a generic algorithm. The company's relationship-driven advisory model, particularly in leasing and capital markets, depends on nuanced local market knowledge, negotiation skill, and trust—attributes where AI augments rather than replaces the human element.

Durable Competitive Moat

CWK possesses a meaningful moat rooted in its global scale, diversified service platform, and entrenched client relationships. Over 53% of the firm's fee revenue comes from Property, Facilities & Project Management (PFPM)—a contract-based, annuity-like business secured through multi-year agreements on a fixed-fee or cost-plus basis. This recurring revenue base provides substantial earnings visibility and creates high switching costs, as institutional occupiers and investors are reluctant to transition the management of large, mission-critical real estate portfolios to untested providers. In the transactional businesses (leasing and capital markets), barriers to entry are equally formidable: brand reputation, regulatory compliance, deep broker networks, and the ability to respond to multinational RFP standards all favor incumbents.

A Consolidated Industry Favoring Scale

The global CRE services industry is highly consolidated, dominated by three firms—CBRE (CBRE), JLL (JLL), and Cushman & Wakefield—that collectively command the vast majority

of institutional mandates and cross-border transactions. This oligopolistic structure creates significant advantages for incumbents: large corporate occupiers and institutional investors strongly prefer working with firms that can service their portfolios across geographies and asset classes from a single platform. For a new entrant, replicating the global infrastructure, talent bench, and data capabilities of these established players would require enormous capital and years of investment. While technology-enabled platforms like CoStar (CSGP) and VTS have carved out niche roles in data and analytics, they have not penetrated the advisory, brokerage, or management businesses where CWK's full-service value proposition is most difficult to displace. The consolidated nature of the industry also provides pricing discipline and limits the risk of destructive competition, which supports margin stability over the cycle.

Compelling Valuation With Multiple Catalysts

At its December 2025 Investor Day, Cushman & Wakefield outlined a 2026–2028 financial framework that includes 6–8% organic fee revenue growth, 15–20% annual adjusted EPS growth, 60–80% free cash flow conversion, 150 bps of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion by year-end 2028, and net leverage of 2x by year-end 2028. Management also said that this framework should translate into roughly $800 million of cumulative free cash flow through 2028, supporting continued deleveraging and reinvestment. In its February 2026 full-year 2025 earnings release, the company reported adjusted EBITDA of $656.2 million, free cash flow of $293.0 million, and noted that it prepaid $300 million of debt during 2025. Not only does CWK trade at a significant discount to peers but also a discount to its historical valuation, while in our view business is improving.

data.title: Riverwater Small Cap Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy Overview / Performance Summary

The Riverwater Partners Small Cap Core Strategy meaningfully outperformed the Russell 2000 Index in the first quarter of 2026. It was the kind of quarter that rewards patience and discipline — and the sort of outcome that feels overdue after extended periods where what we consider quality takes a backseat to speculation.

The first quarter of 2026 was defined by a meaningful shift in market leadership and risk perception, driven primarily by escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and evolving concerns around the durability of certain growth narratives. The outbreak of conflict involving Iran introduced a significant exogenous shock, most visibly through higher energy prices and increased volatility across global markets. While energy markets have historically responded to geopolitical events with short-term spikes, we believe the current environment may reflect a more structural repricing of supply risk, with implications for inflation, corporate margins, and consumer purchasing power. At the same time, equity markets began to reassess areas of prior strength, particularly within software and AI-linked businesses, where concerns have emerged around long-term monetization, competitive intensity, and the risk of overearning relative to sustainable demand.

In parallel, we are observing pockets of stress within private credit markets, driven more by liquidity constraints than underlying credit deterioration. As capital flows have moderated and financing conditions have tightened, certain borrowers—particularly those reliant on continuous access to private capital—are experiencing pressure. Importantly, we view this as a contained dynamic rather than a systemic risk, more akin to the liquidity-driven dislocations observed during the regional banking crisis in 2023 than a broad-based credit unwind. While this environment may create challenges for select companies, it is also contributing to more rational pricing of risk and capital.

Against this backdrop, we are increasingly focused on identifying dislocations created by what we view as indiscriminate selling, particularly in areas exposed to AI-related concerns. In several cases, valuations have compressed meaningfully despite limited change in underlying fundamentals, creating attractive entry points in high-quality businesses with durable growth profiles.

More broadly, periods of heightened uncertainty and liquidity-driven volatility have historically provided compelling opportunities for long-term investors, and we believe the current environment is beginning to present a similar setup.

Sector Highlights

Our overweight positions in Industrials, Materials, Consumer Staples, and Energy all contributed positively, while an underweight to Health Care was additive. The primary headwind came from Financials, where stock selection weighed on results despite a roughly in-line sector weighting.

Information Technology represented our largest sector attribution performer of the portfolio. Stock selection within the sector was the primary driver of a strong positive attribution effect, as several of our technology holdings delivered double-digit returns. Industrials, our second-largest absolute holding, also contributed meaningfully as our second top contributor to performance. Both of our top sectors were driven by stock selection.

Energy was the standout on an absolute return basis — our energy names returned nearly 30% on average during the quarter, driven by favorable commodity dynamics and strong company-specific execution. Meanwhile, our overweight to Consumer Staples and Materials paid off handsomely, with both sectors generating double-digit portfolio returns against low-single-digit benchmark gains. These were not macro bets; they were the result of owning specific businesses we believe are mispriced.

The drag came primarily from Financials , where our holdings declined roughly 6.4% while the benchmark's financial names were down less than 1%. Stock selection in this sector accounted for the vast majority of the negative attribution effect.

Top Contributors & Detractors

Top Contributors

Among our largest contributors, Modine Manufacturing (MOD) led the way during the quarter. The thermal management specialist continued to benefit from secular demand in data center cooling and a well received spinoff of their auto cooling business into a combined company with Gentherm (THRM), with the stock appreciating over 60% as the market recognized the durability of its growth drivers. We have held Modine since early in the strategy's life and trimmed modestly during the quarter, but it remains a meaningful position.

Adeia (ADEA) , an intellectual property licensing business, was the second-largest contributor. Adeia's strong stock performance in the first quarter of 2026 was driven by a combination of fundamental execution, increasing visibility around its licensing model, and a series of high-profile agreements that reinforced the value of its intellectual property portfolio. Coming out of 2025, the company reported record revenue and profitability, supported by a surge in IP licensing activity.

This improving sentiment was further reinforced by several strategic announcements during the quarter. Most notably, Adeia signed a multi-year licensing agreement with AMD (AMD) tied to its hybrid bonding semiconductor technology—an area viewed as critical to next-generation chip architecture—as well as expanded partnerships with UMC and earlier agreements with Disney (DIS) and Microsoft (MSFT).

Top Detractors

Coastal Financial (CCB) was our largest detractor. CCB experienced a more volatile first quarter, with stock performance shaped by evolving investor perceptions around risks tied to its fintech partner ecosystem and broader private credit dynamics. While underlying fundamentals remained relatively stable—supported by its Banking-as-a-Service model and continued growth in partner-driven deposits and fee income—investors grew more focused on the durability of its fintech relationships amid increasing AI-driven disruption and tightened credit conditions. We reduced our position during the quarter but continue to hold CCB at a lower weighting, as we believe the core business model remains intact.

AtriCure (ATRC) underperformed during the first quarter of 2026 despite delivering a strong earnings report and providing 2026 guidance that came in above street expectations, reflecting continued momentum across its core atrial fibrillation and appendage management businesses. The stock's weakness was driven less by fundamentals and more by a shift in investor sentiment following Edwards Lifesciences' (EW) announcement of a competing surgical left atrial appendage closure (LAAC) product, which will directly compete with AtriCure's AtriClip franchise. This development created an overhang on the shares, as investors reassessed the durability of AtriCure's market leadership in appendage management. However, Edwards' product is not expected to be fully available until late in 2026 or 2027, while AtriCure's AtriClip is already well established and widely adopted among cardiac surgeons, supported by strong clinical data and entrenched physician relationships. In our view, the sell-off reflects near-term competitive concerns rather than a deterioration in the company's underlying growth trajectory, and AtriCure remains well positioned given its first-mover advantage and deep integration within surgical workflows.

Strategy Additions & Sales

We were active during the quarter, initiating several new positions and exiting or trimming others where the risk-reward had shifted.

New Positions

We initiated positions in several companies during the quarter that align with our focus on quality businesses trading at attractive valuations.

We initiated a position in Talkspace (TALK) , a digital mental health platform with improving unit economics. TALK was taken out shortly after we made the purchase, providing an immediate return. The acquisition was bittersweet: while the takeout delivered strong performance on the day, we believed the company's improving fundamentals offered more upside over a longer horizon.

Perella Weinberg Partners (PWP) is an advisory-focused investment bank trading at a discount to peers. PWP continues to benefit from a more constructive M&A environment, with a growing backlog that provides increasing visibility into future fee realization. The firm served as advisor on Abbott's (ABT) acquisition of Penumbra (PEN) during the quarter — a transaction that should generate a meaningful fee and, for a smaller-cap advisory platform, has an outsized impact on earnings. However, our timing has been challenged, as escalating geopolitical tensions tied to the Iran conflict have weighed on sentiment around deal activity. We remain constructive on the longer-term outlook, as backlog conversion and continued deal flow should support earnings power as conditions stabilize.

We established a position in Cushman & Wakefield (CWK), which we discuss in greater detail in the Holdings Deep Dive section below. In short, we believe AI-driven fears have created an attractive entry point in a high-quality commercial real estate services franchise trading at a significant discount to peers.

Interface (TILE) is a global leader in sustainable flooring solutions, with its carbon-neutral carpet tile program and use of recycled and bio-based materials positioning it as a preferred supplier in an era of growing ESG procurement standards. Financially, the business is leveraged to a recovery in commercial construction and renovation activity, with margin expansion potential from mix shift toward higher-value resilient flooring and continued operational efficiencies.

We initially identified a turnaround prospect in the luxury outerwear brand Canada Goose (GOOS) , noting positive customer engagement trends. Despite a significant sales beat that demonstrated strong brand resonance, the company reported higher-than-expected SG&A expenses, causing the stock to retreat. When coupled with the energy price spike resulting from the Iran conflict and its subsequent impact on consumer sentiment, we determined that the fundamental backdrop for GOOS had shifted materially, leading us to exit the position.

Position Exits

We liquidated our position in SunOpta (STKL) following its acquisition by an industry peer. We also exited Uranium Energy (UEC), as the original investment thesis had been realized and the risk-reward profile shifted unfavorably. Our stake in Bowhead Specialty (BOW) was sold due to anticipated headwinds resulting from declining short-term interest rates. We chose to consolidate our insurance exposure into Palomar Holdings (PLMR) , which we believe offers superior long-term growth prospects.

We divested from Tennant Company (TNC) during the quarter following a significant ERP system implementation failure that halted customer orders for a three-week period. With the system still not fully recovered, we remain concerned about the potential for strained client relationships or the necessity of offering discounts to retain major accounts.

We trimmed Black Hills Corp (BKH) , Grand Canyon Education (LOPE) , and Warby Parker (WRBY) during the quarter. We also trimmed Modine Manufacturing (MOD) following its strong run, though it remains a meaningful position, and reduced our exposure to Coastal Financial (CCB) , which we discussed in the Detractors section.

Holdings Deep Dive: Cushman & Wakefield (CWK)

An AI-Driven Selloff Creates Opportunity

Cushman & Wakefield (CWK) is one of the world's largest commercial real estate (CRE) services firms, operating in over 60 countries with approximately 52,000 employees. The company provides a comprehensive suite of services—leasing, capital markets advisory, property and facilities management, and valuation—to both occupiers and investors across the office, industrial, retail, life sciences, and data center sectors. Despite strong operational fundamentals and an improving earnings trajectory, CWK shares have traded under persistent pressure due to market concerns that artificial intelligence and technology-driven platforms could disintermediate traditional CRE service providers. We believe this fear is significantly overstated. CEO Michelle MacKay has emphasized that Cushman's key differentiator lies in delivering "tailored, bespoke solutions" through deep client relationships and human expertise—capabilities that cannot be easily replicated by a generic algorithm. The company's relationship-driven advisory model, particularly in leasing and capital markets, depends on nuanced local market knowledge, negotiation skill, and trust—attributes where AI augments rather than replaces the human element.

Durable Competitive Moat

CWK possesses a meaningful moat rooted in its global scale, diversified service platform, and entrenched client relationships. Over 53% of the firm's fee revenue comes from Property, Facilities & Project Management (PFPM)—a contract-based, annuity-like business secured through multi-year agreements on a fixed-fee or cost-plus basis. This recurring revenue base provides substantial earnings visibility and creates high switching costs, as institutional occupiers and investors are reluctant to transition the management of large, mission-critical real estate portfolios to untested providers. In the transactional businesses (leasing and capital markets), barriers to entry are equally formidable: brand reputation, regulatory compliance, deep broker networks, and the ability to respond to multinational RFP standards all favor incumbents.

A Consolidated Industry Favoring Scale

The global CRE services industry is highly consolidated, dominated by three firms—CBRE (CBRE), JLL, and Cushman & Wakefield—that collectively command the vast majority

of institutional mandates and cross-border transactions. This oligopolistic structure creates significant advantages for incumbents: large corporate occupiers and institutional investors strongly prefer working with firms that can service their portfolios across geographies and asset classes from a single platform. For a new entrant, replicating the global infrastructure, talent bench, and data capabilities of these established players would require enormous capital and years of investment. While technology-enabled platforms like CoStar (CSGP) and VTS have carved out niche roles in data and analytics, they have not penetrated the advisory, brokerage, or management businesses where CWK's full-service value proposition is most difficult to displace. The consolidated nature of the industry also provides pricing discipline and limits the risk of destructive competition, which supports margin stability over the cycle.

Compelling Valuation With Multiple Catalysts

At its December 2025 Investor Day, Cushman & Wakefield outlined a 2026–2028 financial framework that includes 6–8% organic fee revenue growth, 15–20% annual adjusted EPS growth, 60–80% free cash flow conversion, 150 bps of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion by year-end 2028, and net leverage of 2x by year-end 2028. Management also said that this framework should translate into roughly $800 million of cumulative free cash flow through 2028, supporting continued deleveraging and reinvestment. In its February 2026 full-year 2025 earnings release, the company reported adjusted EBITDA of $656.2 million, free cash flow of $293.0 million, and noted that it prepaid $300 million of debt during 2025. Not only does CWK trade at a significant discount to peers but also a discount to its historical valuation, while in our view business is improving.

Outlook

Looking ahead to 2026, we entered the year with a constructive view on small-cap equities, supported by improving monetary conditions, attractive relative valuations, and an anticipated rotation back toward higher-quality businesses. As discussed in our prior commentary, we believed easing financial conditions and accelerating earnings growth would create a favorable backdrop for cyclically oriented sectors and companies positioned for fundamental inflection. However, the recent escalation of geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, specifically the onset of conflict involving Iran, has meaningfully altered the near-term macro landscape and introduced a higher degree of uncertainty into both economic and market outlooks.

The most immediate impact has been a sharp increase in oil and broader energy prices, which we expect will act as a tax on the consumer and weigh on discretionary spending. Tax refunds are trending stronger than last year and would typically have provided a near-term tailwind to consumption, but we expect much of that incremental liquidity to be absorbed by higher energy and gasoline costs. This dynamic, combined with tariffs that remain in place and the growing likelihood that inflation reaccelerates in the coming months, reduces the probability and magnitude of near-term rate cuts.

As a result, areas of the market we previously viewed as poised to inflect—particularly consumer-oriented businesses—may face a more prolonged recovery path. While the long-term fundamentals for many of these companies remain intact, the timing of earnings acceleration has become less certain, warranting a more cautious near-term stance.

In response, we have begun repositioning the portfolio toward areas that are better aligned with the current environment. We see increasing opportunity in energy and materials, which we believe stand to benefit from supply disruptions and sustained higher commodity prices. At the same time, we are incrementally adding to more defensive sectors such as utilities and real estate, where cash flow visibility and relative insulation from economic volatility are more attractive. Importantly, this shift does not represent a departure from our core philosophy of owning high-quality businesses with durable competitive advantages, but rather a recalibration of where we see the most compelling risk-adjusted opportunities today.

Despite the heightened uncertainty, we would emphasize that markets tend to respond to changes at the margin. Similar to the most recent prior periods of disruption—including COVID, the 2022 rate shock, and the regional banking crisis—conditions do not need to fully normalize for equities to recover. Instead, stabilization and incremental improvement are typically sufficient to support a re-rating in risk assets.

We are closely monitoring developments in the Middle East and remain prepared to selectively add to high-quality businesses that have been indiscriminately sold and are trading at more attractive valuations. Over time, we believe this disciplined approach will allow us to capitalize on dislocations while maintaining a strong focus on long-term capital compounding.

We remain committed to our disciplined, quality-oriented approach. The first quarter served as a reminder that owning profitable, well-managed businesses purchased at reasonable valuations can and does work, even when the broader narrative favors something else entirely. We are grateful for your trust and confidence.

Nathan Fredrick, CFAPortfolio Manager
